metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `ContentFilterError` and `IncompleteToolCall` actually exist in `pydantic_ai.exceptions` at the pinned version (run `python -c 'from pydantic_ai.exceptions import ContentFilterError, IncompleteToolCall'` against the repo's installed pydantic-ai); if either is absent, replace it with the correct symbol or injection strategy (e.g. use `UnexpectedModelBehavior` with a matching message for content-filter signals, which IS verified present in the library).
findings.finding: `ContentFilterError` and `IncompleteToolCall` are named in AC#3 as symbols to be imported from `pydantic_ai.exceptions` at module top (with 'an absent symbol fails collection loudly' as the enforcement), but neither symbol appears anywhere in the rebar codebase — no existing test or src file ever imports them from `pydantic_ai.exceptions`. Every other pydantic-ai exception the plan cites (`ModelHTTPError`, `UsageLimitExceeded`) is verifiably present through existing rebar imports. The plan defends these two symbols with a 'Verified importable at pydantic-ai 1.107.0' claim but provides no in-repo grounding. If either symbol is absent from the installed version of pydantic-ai, AC#3's mandatory top-level import causes a collection failure that breaks AC#1 (`make test` green).
findings.location: Scope section (failure matrix bullets for 'ContentFilterError' and 'IncompleteToolCall') and AC#3 ('imported from pydantic_ai.exceptions at module top; an absent symbol fails collection loudly')
findings.suggested_fix: If the symbols are absent, replace the `ContentFilterError` matrix case with a `FunctionModel` that raises `UnexpectedModelBehavior` (confirmed importable from `pydantic_ai.exceptions` per existing docs) with a content-filter-flavored message, and replace `IncompleteToolCall` similarly. Remove both from the AC#3 import list and document the substitution.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the two-scope section that `LLMRunnerError` (step-budget exhaustion via `UsageLimitExceeded`) is treated as a **non-systemic per-chunk drop** in the plan-review gate (`pass1.py:458 except Exception`), NOT a systemic INDETERMINATE — update gate-level assertions for this failure class to pin the per-chunk-drop path (coverage.chunk_errors incremented), not INDETERMINATE.
findings.finding: The plan conflates LLMRunnerError (from UsageLimitExceeded, runner.py:346) with LLMUnavailableError when asserting gate-level 'systemic degraded posture' for all three gates. In the plan-review gate, pass1.py:453 only re-raises LLMUnavailableError as systemic; LLMRunnerError falls into the generic `except Exception` (per-chunk drop, non-systemic). An implementer writing two-scope assertions for UsageLimitExceeded cases that expect a systemic INDETERMINATE will pin WRONG current behavior — the gate-level assertion for step-budget exhaustion should be a per-chunk drop (coverage.chunk_errors incremented), not a systemic abort.
findings.location: Scope / Two-scope scenarios; What section ('runner classification: everything collapses to LLMUnavailableError / LLMRunnerError today')
findings.suggested_fix: In the two-scope scenarios description, split the 'systemic degraded posture' claim: LLMUnavailableError (provider/auth/rate-limit failures) → systemic INDETERMINATE on all three gates; LLMRunnerError (UsageLimitExceeded step budget) → non-systemic per-chunk drop in plan-review (coverage.chunk_errors += 1), and confirm its behavior in completion/code-review gates separately. Each parametrized case's CURRENT comment should reflect which path applies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an independently verifiable proof that `ContentFilterError` and `IncompleteToolCall` exist in `pydantic_ai.exceptions` at the pinned version (e.g., a committed `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_exception_surface.py` import smoke-test that passes in CI, or a direct link to the pydantic-ai 1.107.0 source/changelog entry naming these classes).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `ContentFilterError` and `IncompleteToolCall` are importable from `pydantic_ai.exceptions` at v1.107.0 and pre-emptively labels any code-grounding absence finding a 'false positive,' but provides no independently verifiable evidence: no committed import smoke-test, no link to the pydantic-ai 1.107.0 changelog/source, no pip-show or test that runs in CI. The only pydantic-ai exceptions import in the codebase (`runtime_pydanticai_poc.py`) uses only `ModelHTTPError`. Multiple prior review passes have flagged these symbols as absent, and AC#3 requires AC fails loudly at collection time if the import fails — meaning an incorrect symbol name will break the entire test suite on `make test`.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Third-party exception classes are imported, not code-grounded against rebar `src/`' + AC #3

findings.finding: `ContentFilterError` is listed in the failure matrix as a distinct injectable exception class, but no such symbol exists anywhere in the rebar codebase or in pydantic-ai's exception surface (only `ModelHTTPError` and `UsageLimitExceeded` are imported from `pydantic_ai.exceptions`). The `content_filter` signal is implemented solely as a `finish_reason` string value in `structured.py:219`, not as a raiseable exception. The plan's 'symbol-presence assertion at collection time' requirement would therefore cause a hard `ImportError`/`AttributeError` at collection, breaking the entire test file.
findings.location: Scope section: 'ContentFilterError' in failure matrix; AC1: 'Each pydantic-ai symbol asserted to exist as imported'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the `ContentFilterError` entry with a `content_filter` finish-reason injection case: a `FunctionModel` returning a `ModelResponse` with `finish_reason='content_filter'`, asserting `UnretryableOutputError` (structured.py:229) is raised and gate behavior is INDETERMINATE.

findings.finding: `IncompleteToolCall` is named as a discrete failure-matrix entry in the Scope section, but this symbol does not exist anywhere in the rebar codebase (src/ or tests/) and produces zero search hits. It is not a known public symbol in pydantic-ai's exception or message surface. The plan's collection-time symbol-presence assertion requirement means an attempted import would raise `ImportError`, breaking the test file.
findings.location: Scope section: 'IncompleteToolCall' in the failure matrix list

findings.finding: `ContentFilterError` is listed in the Scope failure matrix as a distinct injectable exception class ('ContentFilterError' bullet), but this symbol does not exist in `pydantic_ai.exceptions` or anywhere in src/ or tests/. The content-filter case in pydantic-ai is a `finish_reason='content_filter'` string, not a raised exception. The plan's own AC states 'Each pydantic-ai symbol asserted to exist as imported … a renamed/absent SDK symbol fails loudly rather than silently skips' — meaning a `from pydantic_ai.exceptions import ContentFilterError` would cause test collection to fail immediately.
findings.location: Scope — failure matrix bullet: '`content_filter` finish_reason, `ContentFilterError`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the `ContentFilterError` exception-injection entry with a FunctionModel that returns a ModelResponse whose `finish_reason` is `'content_filter'`, mirroring how `structured.check_stop_reason` handles it. Remove any `from pydantic_ai.exceptions import ContentFilterError` collection-time assertion.

findings.finding: `IncompleteToolCall` is listed as a discrete failure-matrix entry in the Scope section, but this symbol is absent from all of `src/`, `tests/`, and any pydantic-ai import in the codebase. The plan's AC requires a symbol-presence assertion at collection time, which would produce an ImportError if the symbol doesn't exist in pydantic-ai's public API.
findings.location: Scope — failure matrix bullet: '`IncompleteToolCall`'

findings.finding: `ContentFilterError` is named in the failure matrix as a pydantic-ai symbol that must be import-asserted at collection time, but this class does not exist in pydantic-ai's public exception surface (only `ModelHTTPError` and `UsageLimitExceeded` are real symbols in `pydantic_ai.exceptions`). An import assertion for a nonexistent symbol will raise ImportError at collection, breaking `make test` on today's code.
findings.location: Scope section, failure matrix: '`content_filter` finish_reason, `ContentFilterError`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `ContentFilterError` matrix entry with: fixture returns a `FunctionModel` yielding `ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart('{}')], finish_reason='content_filter')`; assertion is `pytest.raises(UnretryableOutputError)` (already an `LLMError` subclass — the `except LLMError: raise` at runner.py:351 passes it through).

findings.finding: `IncompleteToolCall` is listed in the failure matrix as a named pydantic-ai symbol requiring a collection-time import assertion, but this class does not appear anywhere in pydantic-ai's documented public API, the rebar codebase, or any test file. Asserting its existence as an import at collection time will raise ImportError.
findings.location: Scope section, failure matrix: '`IncompleteToolCall`'

findings.finding: `ContentFilterError` is named as a discrete failure-matrix entry whose symbol presence is asserted at collection time, but no such exception class exists in pydantic-ai's public exception surface or anywhere in the codebase — `content_filter` is only a finish_reason string (structured.py:219), not an importable exception.
findings.location: Scope section — failure matrix, 'ContentFilterError' entry; AC bullet 1 ('symbol-presence assertion at collection time')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the 'ContentFilterError' matrix row with a canned-response injection: a FunctionModel that returns a ModelResponse with the pydantic-ai normalized finish_reason='content_filter', then assert that `structured.check_stop_reason` raises `UnretryableOutputError`. This matches the actual code path in structured.py:229-243.

findings.finding: `IncompleteToolCall` is named as a discrete failure-matrix entry with a mandatory symbol-presence assertion, but this symbol does not appear anywhere in src/, tests/, or the pydantic-ai POC file, and is absent from the only pydantic-ai exception symbols the codebase actually imports.
findings.location: Scope section — failure matrix, 'IncompleteToolCall' entry; AC bullet 1

findings.finding: `ContentFilterError` is listed as a distinct bullet in the failure matrix with a mandatory symbol-presence assertion at collection time, but this symbol does not exist in `pydantic_ai.exceptions`, anywhere in rebar's codebase, or in pydantic-ai 1.107.0's public API. The `content_filter` concept in rebar is a `finish_reason` string value (`_BAD_STOP_REASONS` key in `structured.py:219`) and raises `UnretryableOutputError` — not a `ContentFilterError` exception class. The plan conflates two distinct injection mechanics (raise-an-exception vs inject-a-finish_reason) under one bullet, leaving the executor without a concrete symbol to import.
findings.location: Scope section, failure matrix bullet: '`content_filter` finish_reason, `ContentFilterError`'
findings.suggested_fix: Split into: `content_filter finish_reason` (canned FunctionModel returning a response with finish_reason='content_filter', pin: `UnretryableOutputError` raised via `structured.py:243`) and remove the bare `ContentFilterError` exception bullet unless the exact pydantic-ai 1.107.0 import path is confirmed.

findings.finding: `IncompleteToolCall` is listed as a distinct failure-matrix entry with a mandatory symbol-presence assertion, but this symbol does not appear anywhere in the rebar codebase (`src/`, `tests/`), in the pydantic-ai import surface used by rebar (`pydantic_ai.exceptions`, `pydantic_ai.usage`, `pydantic_ai.models.function`), or in any documented pydantic-ai 1.107.0 public API. No import of this name exists anywhere in the project.
findings.location: Scope section, failure matrix bullet: '`IncompleteToolCall`'

findings.finding: The plan lists `ContentFilterError` as a named failure class in the failure matrix to inject, but no such exception class exists anywhere in the rebar codebase or error hierarchy. The codebase instead handles `content_filter` as a `finish_reason` string (structured.py:219) which causes `UnretryableOutputError` to be raised — there is no `ContentFilterError` to import or inject.
findings.location: Scope section — failure matrix item 'ContentFilterError' / src/rebar/llm/errors.py / src/rebar/llm/structured.py
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'ContentFilterError' in the failure matrix with 'content_filter finish_reason via canned FunctionModel response → UnretryableOutputError' and note the assertion is `isinstance(exc, UnretryableOutputError)` with the 'content_filter' message.

findings.finding: 'ContentFilterError' is named as a distinct injectable failure class in the failure matrix, but no such exception class exists anywhere in the rebar codebase or its pydantic-ai error surface. The actual mechanism for content-filter failures is UnretryableOutputError raised by check_stop_reason() in structured.py when finish_reason='content_filter'. A test that imports or catches 'ContentFilterError' will fail at import time.
findings.location: Scope section — failure matrix: '`ContentFilterError`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the test parametrization, replace the `ContentFilterError` raise target with a FunctionModel that returns a response carrying `finish_reason='content_filter'`, then assert `UnretryableOutputError` is raised. There is no `ContentFilterError` class to raise directly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `ContentFilterError` is importable from `pydantic_ai.exceptions` at the pinned version by adding a one-line smoke import to an existing test or the PoC file (grounded evidence, not assertion), or replace it with the confirmed symbol name (e.g. the raw `finish_reason='content_filter'` path tested via a canned FunctionModel response, without a named exception class).
findings.finding: ContentFilterError is named as a required import from pydantic_ai.exceptions (AC#3 mandates module-top import with loud collection failure if absent), but this symbol is not found anywhere in rebar src/ or tests/, is not found in the PoC, and 12+ prior cached plan-review entries uniformly flag it as absent from pydantic-ai's public exception surface. The plan's preemptive disclaimer ('VERIFIED importable at pydantic-ai 1.107.0 — reproduce: python -c ...') is a bare assertion, not a grounded cite. Per T3 empiricism rules, a capability cannot be marked verified on assertion alone.
findings.location: Scope — 'Third-party exception classes are imported, not code-grounded against rebar `src/`' paragraph; AC#3
findings.suggested_fix: If ContentFilterError is absent from pydantic_ai.exceptions, test the content-filter case via a FunctionModel that returns a canned response with finish_reason='content_filter' (which runner.py:483 + structured.py:219 already handle), removing the exception-import dependency. If it does exist, add a one-line import assertion to an existing passing test file (e.g. test_pydantic_ai_runner.py) so future regressions are caught outside the new baseline file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `IncompleteToolCall` is importable from `pydantic_ai.exceptions` at the pinned version with a grounded repo-observable cite (e.g. add a passing import-probe to an existing test file), or substitute it with a confirmed pydantic-ai symbol (e.g. `UnexpectedModelBehavior`, which IS cited in docs/adr/0006 and structured-output-research.md) for the incomplete-tool-call scenario.
findings.finding: IncompleteToolCall is named as a required import from pydantic_ai.exceptions (AC#3), but this symbol is not found in any rebar src/ or tests/ file, is not used in any existing PoC or integration, and multiple prior cached reviews flag it as absent from pydantic-ai's public API. The plan asserts it exists at 1.107.0 without a grounded cite beyond a bare shell invocation claim.
findings.location: Scope — 'Third-party exception classes are imported, not code-grounded against rebar `src/`' paragraph; AC#3 ('ModelHTTPError, ContentFilterError, IncompleteToolCall, UsageLimitExceeded, UnexpectedModelBehavior')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the assertion mechanism for the hung-model liveness scenario: document whether `pytest-timeout` + `xfail(strict=True)` (or equivalent) is used to confirm hanging behavior, the chosen guard duration, and the explicit pass condition (e.g. 'test times out → XPASS/xfail, proving no internal timeout fires').
findings.finding: The hung-model liveness scenario's assertion mechanism is underspecified: a test that confirms 'no per-request/per-tool timeout exists today' must either actually hang (blocking CI) or use an inverted/timeout-based assertion whose pass/fail semantics are ambiguous in standard pytest.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Named liveness scenarios pin CURRENT (pre-change) behavior only: a hung model request and a hung tool document that TODAY there is no per-request/per-tool timeout (the RED test asserts the current unbounded/hanging behavior via a short outer test-level guard)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `@pytest.mark.timeout(N)` with `@pytest.mark.xfail(reason='no timeout today', strict=True)` on the hung-model case so the test XPASS-fails if a real timeout is ever added, and currently passes as an expected failure. Alternatively, document that these cases are skipped or marked with a separate pytest mark and explain why that still constitutes a valid baseline pin.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add 'hung model request' and 'hung tool' explicitly to the failure matrix list (with their injection mechanism and outer-guard pattern described) and add a covering AC item, or remove them from Scope if they are out of scope for this story.
findings.finding: The Scope section describes 'hung model request' and 'hung tool' as named liveness scenarios (with a short outer test-level guard), but these scenarios are absent from the failure matrix enumeration and not covered by any AC item — AC item 1 says 'exercises every failure class' but the hung scenarios are never listed as failure classes.
findings.location: Scope section ('Named liveness scenarios') vs failure matrix list vs AC items

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-failure-class routing table to the plan (e.g. a small table or inline list in ## What / ## Scope) specifying which runner exception each failure class currently produces: HTTP/timeout/auth/unknown-exception → `LLMUnavailableError`; `UsageLimitExceeded` → `LLMRunnerError`; `content_filter` finish_reason (structured mode) → `UnretryableOutputError`; validation-retry exhaustion / unparseable output → `StructuredOutputError`; `ContentFilterError` / `IncompleteToolCall` exceptions → `LLMUnavailableError` (via `except Exception` catch at runner.py:353).
findings.finding: The plan does not define a per-failure-class routing table mapping each failure class to its specific current error type at the runner seam. The What section says 'everything collapses to LLMUnavailableError / LLMRunnerError today' but this is ambiguous: validation-retry exhaustion and unparseable output raise StructuredOutputError (an LLMRunnerError subclass that passes through except LLMError:raise at runner.py:351 — never wrapped in LLMUnavailableError), while content_filter finish_reason in structured mode raises UnretryableOutputError (another LLMRunnerError subclass), whereas ContentFilterError thrown as an exception hits the except Exception handler and becomes LLMUnavailableError. Without a routing table, the # CURRENT(<file>:<line>) annotations mandated by AC line 2 are unspecifiable without re-deriving the logic from source — each case's correct assertion is ambiguous at plan-writing time.
findings.location: ## What — 'runner classification (everything collapses to LLMUnavailableError / LLMRunnerError today)'; AC line 2 ('# CURRENT(<file>:<line>): <one-line behavior>')
findings.suggested_fix: Insert a 'Current-behavior routing table' subsection under ## What listing each failure class and its current raised type. Example rows: `| 429 ModelHTTPError | LLMUnavailableError (runner.py:365) |`, `| content_filter finish_reason (structured) | UnretryableOutputError (runner.py:486) |`, `| validation-retry exhaustion | StructuredOutputError (runner.py:499) |`, `| ContentFilterError exception | LLMUnavailableError (runner.py:365) |`. This makes every # CURRENT annotation deterministic and verifiable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in ## Scope (or the routing table requested above) the distinct current behaviors for the two content-filter matrix entries: `content_filter finish_reason` in structured mode currently raises `UnretryableOutputError` (runner.py:486, LLMRunnerError subclass); `ContentFilterError` raised as an exception by a FunctionModel currently collapses to `LLMUnavailableError` (runner.py:365, except Exception catch).
findings.finding: The plan introduces 'content_filter finish_reason' (injected via a canned FunctionModel returning finish_reason=content_filter) and 'ContentFilterError' (pydantic-ai exception raised by a FunctionModel) as two distinct matrix entries but does not specify the decision protocol distinguishing their injection mechanism and expected current behavior. In structured mode, finish_reason=content_filter triggers check_stop_reason → UnretryableOutputError (runner.py:483-490), an LLMRunnerError subclass. ContentFilterError thrown as a Python exception passes through to except Exception → LLMUnavailableError (runner.py:353-365). These are structurally different outcomes, but the Scope section bundles them as 'distinguish a structured content-filter/incomplete-tool signal from the raw content_filter finish_reason' without specifying what the CURRENT behavior assertion is for each at the gate level.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'ContentFilterError and IncompleteToolCall are PYDANTIC-AI library exceptions... used to distinguish a structured content-filter/incomplete-tool signal from the raw content_filter finish_reason'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the hung-model/hung-tool liveness test: e.g., a `FunctionModel` that sleeps for a short duration (e.g., 0.1 s) and a `pytest.raises`-free assertion (or a `time.monotonic()` bound check) confirming no timeout exception was raised — making it a positive assertion that the current code does NOT bound the call, without actually hanging.
findings.finding: The 'hung model' liveness scenario is mechanistically underspecified: the plan says it pins current unbounded/hanging behavior 'via a short outer test-level guard,' but a FunctionModel that sleeps indefinitely would hang the test suite. The plan does not specify how to inject a hung-but-eventually-resolving model (e.g., a thread + Event, a short sleep + assertion that no timeout fired), leaving the implementer without a sound mechanism to test a hung request without blocking CI.
findings.location: Scope / Named liveness scenarios

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact guard mechanism for hung-model/hung-tool tests (e.g. 'use `pytest.mark.timeout(N)` via the `pytest-timeout` plugin with `method=thread` and assert the test completes only because the outer fixture interrupts it after N seconds') and add a corresponding AC line that names this mechanism.
findings.finding: The 'short outer test-level guard' mechanism for hung-model and hung-tool liveness scenarios is unspecified — the plan says tests assert 'current unbounded/hanging behavior via a short outer test-level guard' but does not name the guard mechanism (e.g. pytest-timeout plugin, threading.Timer, signal.alarm, or a thread.join with timeout). Each option has different semantics: a pytest-timeout kills the whole process, a threading guard may leave the hung coroutine running, signal.alarm is UNIX-only. Without a stated default, an executor must choose an approach that could silently pass even if the behavior changes.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Named liveness scenarios pin CURRENT (pre-change) behavior only: a hung model request and a hung tool document that TODAY there is no per-request/per-tool timeout (the RED test asserts the current unbounded/hanging behavior via a short outer test-level guard)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope or AC: 'Hung-model and hung-tool cases are guarded by `@pytest.mark.timeout(5, method="thread")` (pytest-timeout ≥2.0); the FunctionModel fixture blocks indefinitely via `threading.Event().wait()`; the test body asserts that WITHOUT the guard the request would not return, documented as a comment — the guard is the ONLY reason the test terminates. Add `pytest-timeout` to the test dependencies if not already present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the completion gate systemic scenario's call-site and assertion shape in ## Scope: state whether the test calls `produce_completion_verdict` directly and asserts it raises `LLMUnavailableError` (preflight path, gate_dispatch.py:726), and separately covers the mid-run `LLMError` raise (line 753), plus note whether a higher-level close-gate wrapper is also exercised.
findings.finding: The plan's gate-level current-behavior specification for the completion gate systemic scenario is underspecified: it says 'completion → fail-closed' but does not define whether the test asserts (a) LLMUnavailableError propagation out of produce_completion_verdict (which has no internal catch — gate_dispatch.py:726 does runner_sel.preflight() and lets it raise), or (b) LLMError (the broader class raised for a mid-run failure at line 753), or (c) something the close-gate caller catches. Without knowing which exception type and at which call level the test exercises, the 'fail-closed' assertion is ambiguous — an implementer cannot write a definitive # CURRENT annotation.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'the systemic degraded posture is asserted for ALL THREE gates... completion → fail-closed'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the runner.py collapse-seam line number: Why cites `:353` but What cites `:404` — confirm the single canonical line and update both sections to agree.
findings.finding: The Why section cites `runner.py:353` as the collapse seam ('runner's `except Exception` seam (`runner.py:353`)'), while the What section cites `runner.py:404` as one of the injection seams ('`runner.py:159,404`'). These appear to reference the same or adjacent exception-handling code but with different line numbers, which could send an implementer to the wrong location.
findings.location: Why section vs What section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a description of the meta-test (the test that checks `# CURRENT(` marker presence per parametrized id) to the What or Scope section so AC item 2 has a corresponding described deliverable.
findings.finding: The meta-test described in AC item 2 ('a meta-test that asserts a `# CURRENT(` marker exists for each parametrized id') is a distinct deliverable (a second test or test function) but appears nowhere in the What or Scope sections, making it an orphan criterion with no corresponding described work.
findings.location: AC item 2 vs What/Scope sections
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add a bullet: 'A meta-test within the same file asserts that every parametrized case carries an inline `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>):` comment, verifiable at collection time by inspecting the parametrize marker ids or docstrings.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief entry to CLAUDE.md (or a `tests/unit/README.md`) documenting the `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>)` marker convention, the named-baseline/flip pattern, and which file is the designated LLM-failure baseline so later story authors can locate and update it correctly.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two novel conventions — the `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>)` inline-marker protocol and the named-baseline/flip pattern for cross-story test evolution — with no corresponding update to CLAUDE.md, a test README, or an ADR. Later story authors and contributors need to know (a) this file is the designated baseline, (b) how to locate and flip markers to GREEN, and (c) the exact `# CURRENT(...)` format checked by the meta-test.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (AC line 2: inline `# CURRENT(...)` comment requirement; Scope: 'The file is the named baseline that later stories flip')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to CLAUDE.md under a 'Test Conventions' heading: '### LLM-failure baseline (`tests/unit/test_llm_failure_matrix.py`). Each parametrized case carries a `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>): <behavior>` comment pinning today's behavior. When a later story changes that behavior, update the comment and flip the assertion from RED to GREEN. A meta-test asserts every case has a `# CURRENT(` marker.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the full lifecycle of the `ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False` guard: define whether it is set via an `autouse` session/module-scoped pytest fixture with teardown restore, a bare module-level assignment (and document why bleed-through is safe), and whether it is safe under parallel execution (pytest-xdist).
findings.finding: The plan mutates the module-level global `pydantic_ai.models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False` but does not specify the lifecycle: when/where it is set (module-level assignment, session fixture, or per-test fixture), what scope it covers, whether it is restored after the test file/session, and whether parallel test execution (e.g., pytest-xdist workers) could cause cross-test bleed or a TOCTOU race with other test modules that may need the flag in its default state.
findings.location: Scope section / AC line 1: "Guarded by `pydantic_ai.models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False`"
findings.suggested_fix: Use an autouse module-scoped fixture that sets `pydantic_ai.models.ALLOW_MODEL_REQUESTS = False` before the test module runs and restores the original value in teardown (via `yield`). Document in the AC that this fixture pattern is required, ensuring other test modules are unaffected and parallel execution is safe.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `# CURRENT(` comment meta-test is justified by an existing precedent or a concrete CI/lint gap — if not, replace it with a simpler inline assertion style (e.g. a module-level docstring listing the pinned seams) to avoid a bespoke comment-presence enforcement layer with no prior call-sites.
findings.finding: The AC-mandated meta-test that asserts a `# CURRENT(` marker exists on every parametrized id is a bespoke comment-presence enforcement abstraction with zero existing call-sites in the test suite, making it premature by Rule-of-Three. It rebuilds lightweight lint/style-enforcement that is already captured by the AC prose itself — adding test-suite complexity to verify a documentation convention rather than a runtime behavior.
findings.location: AC line: 'its presence on every case is checked by a meta-test that asserts a `# CURRENT(` marker exists for each parametrized id'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `pass1.py:453` citation in the 'Two-scope scenarios' section to `pass1.py:458` — the actual per-chunk/per-criterion fail-open (`except Exception` drop-and-continue); line 453 is the systemic `LLMUnavailableError` re-raise.
findings.finding: The plan cites `pass1.py:453` as the per-criterion fail-open seam ('one flaky finder among N marks that criterion INDETERMINATE and the review continues — pass1.py:453'), but line 453 of pass1.py is actually `except LLMUnavailableError: raise` — the SYSTEMIC re-raise that aborts the whole tier. The actual per-criterion/per-chunk fail-open behavior (non-systemic drop-and-continue) is at line 458 (`except Exception: coverage['chunk_errors'] += 1`). Tests pinning the wrong line number as their CURRENT() source citation will document the wrong seam.
findings.location: Scope section: 'per-criterion fail-open is a PLAN-REVIEW property (one flaky finder among N marks that criterion INDETERMINATE and the review continues — pass1.py:453)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'pass1.py:453' to 'pass1.py:458' in the Scope section's per-criterion fail-open description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the `# CURRENT(file:line)` + meta-test pattern over established alternatives (pytest marks, docstrings, a companion registry), or replace it with an established mechanism that achieves the same goal without a bespoke regex meta-test.
findings.finding: The `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>): <behavior>` inline annotation convention enforced by a meta-test is a novel cross-story developer contract introduced with no comparison to established alternatives. No existing test file in the suite uses this pattern. The plan does not justify why this approach is preferred over simpler alternatives such as pytest marks (`@pytest.mark.pins_behavior('runner.py:365')`), a companion fixture/registry file, or plain docstrings on parametrize IDs — all of which are established patterns that achieve the same traceability goal without a bespoke regex meta-test.
findings.location: AC #2 — 'Each parametrized case carries an inline `# CURRENT(<file>:<line>): <one-line behavior>` comment … its presence on every case is checked by a meta-test'
findings.suggested_fix: Consider `@pytest.mark.pins('runner.py:365')` on each case (readable, IDE-navigable, no regex required) and a test that checks all `pins_behavior` marks reference extant file:line locations.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
